column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Rubber Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/composite/fiber-reinforced/rubber-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for rubber cleaning without thermal damage
Near-infrared wavelength for optimal rubber absorption and minimal substrate damage
Beam spot diameter for precise rubber removal with adequate cleaning area
Optimal repetition rate for efficient rubber cleaning with good process control
Fluence threshold for effective rubber ablation while preserving substrate integrity
Nanosecond pulse duration for effective rubber ablation with controlled thermal effects
Optimal scanning speed for uniform rubber removal without excessive heat accumulation
Recommended number of passes for complete rubber removal with process control
Optimal beam overlap for uniform rubber removal without excessive processing time

Low power to prevent thermal degradation of rubber (low melting point ~100-200°C); typical range from industrial fiber lasers for polymers. Recommended start at 50W for light contamination. Source: IPG Photonics specs for cleaning delicate substrates; academic review in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2018) on polymer ablation.

High speeds minimize heat accumulation on low-thermal-conductivity rubber; slower for heavy contamination (1000-3000 mm/s), faster for light (5000-10000 mm/s). Recommended 5000 mm/s for balance. Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals; case studies in Journal of Cleaner Production (2020) for surface prep.

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective industrial cleaning; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-minimal HAZ but less common for rubber. 20 ns balances ablation of contaminants with low heat input. Source: Laser physics in Applied Surface Science (2019) on polymer cleaning thresholds; ns pulses avoid melting unlike longer durations.

Moderate rates for effective contaminant removal without excessive heat buildup in rubber; higher rates (>50 kHz) speed up process but risk accumulation. Trade-off noted in literature. Source: Academic literature in Lasers in Manufacturing and Materials Processing (2021); typical for 1064 nm fiber lasers from Coherent specs.

Low fluence to stay below rubber damage threshold (~0.5-1 J/cm² for ablation onset); 0.1-0.5 J/cm² for light contaminants, up to 1 J/cm² for heavy. Conservative for safe cleaning. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2017) on elastomers; uncertainty noted for specific rubber types due to variability in composition.

Smaller spots for precise cleaning on rubber surfaces; 100-200 μm for fine detail (e.g., molds), larger for broad areas. 200 μm recommended for uniform coverage without overlap issues. Source: Equipment specifications from Trumpf and Adapt Laser Systems; process optimization in Surface and Coatings Technology (2022).

Multi-pass for thorough removal of stubborn contaminants on rubber; single pass for light soiling, 2-3 for typical industrial prep. Recommended 2 passes to ensure completeness without damage. Source: Industry practice in case studies from Rubber & Plastics News (2020); multi-pass approaches in laser cleaning guidelines from SPIE proceedings.

50% overlap ensures uniform coverage and contaminant removal on irregular rubber surfaces; lower (30%) for speed, higher (70-80%) for heavy contamination. Balances efficiency and evenness. Source: Process optimization studies in Optics and Laser Technology (2019); typical range from manufacturer guidelines like those from CleanLaser.
